name: Accent-Tap Exercise (8th Notes)
description: Alternating accented and unaccented 8th notes — building dynamic contrast

notation.pattern: R l R l R l R l

teacher_notes.beginner: The accented notes should be 4–5x louder than the taps. Taps are nearly whispered.
teacher_notes.intermediate: Move the accent to different positions — beat 2, beat 3, beat 4 — to break patterns.
teacher_notes.advanced: The same control principle applies everywhere. Every note you play is either an accent or a tap.

name: Ghost Note Groove
description: Developing consistent, controlled ghost notes woven into a groove

teacher_notes.beginner: Ghost notes are like whispers between the backbeats. They should be felt more than heard.
teacher_notes.intermediate: Keep your left hand very close to the head for ghost notes. The rebound does the work.
teacher_notes.advanced: Listen to David Garibaldi or Steve Jordan for how ghost notes define a funk groove's personality.

name: Crescendo/Decrescendo Singles
description: Single stroke roll that gradually increases then decreases in volume

teacher_notes.beginner: Start at pp (very soft) and climb to ff (very loud) over 4 bars, then back down. Use stick height as your volume dial.
teacher_notes.intermediate: The climb should be smooth, not stepped. Like a dimmer switch, not an on/off.
teacher_notes.advanced: Do this exercise in the dark, listening only. Your ears will tell you if the arc is even.
